insight: Buying Mobile Animuses to generate temporary red for Vai Mauronax, then clicking them on the same turn as buying Vai to convert to Rhinos, is likely the optimal timing for this combo. The extra cost of Mobile Animus (4 vs 3 for a regular Animus) is relatively minor (~0.6 extra gold for 2 red), but the main drawback is being left with Rhinos, which are considered a weak unit.
author: consensus
context: velizar_ and shadourow discussing the economic trade-offs of using Mobile Animuses to enable Vai Mauronax purchase while minimizing leftover red infrastructure.

insight: Rhino is widely considered a weak unit, with its fair value estimated around 5 gold rather than its actual cost. Grimbotch at 5 gold is considered better than Rhino because it attacks one additional time, suggesting Rhino is overpriced even at 5.
author: velizar_
context: Discussion of Mobile Animus/Vai combo costs, leading to analysis of Rhino's inherent weakness as the main cost of the strategy.

insight: Rhino's theoretical maximum value (defending 2 threat, absorbing, attacking twice, and dying) would make it worth at least 6 gold, but in practice you almost never get full value from Rhino, confirming it is generally a weak unit in real games.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Follow-up thread evaluating Rhino's theoretical vs practical value after the Mobile Animus/Vai discussion.

insight: The standard Deadeye Operative opening for Player 2 is DD DD DBB 1DDE. Because Deadeye is typically bought in low-inflation sets, the presence of Flame Animus in the set does not significantly affect the opening for many turns, so players should follow the standard Deadeye opening regardless.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of how to handle a Flame Animus set when Deadeye Operative is the primary strategy, with recohou26799 proposing early DEO and amalloy confirming the standard opening.

insight: A tempo Deadeye Operative purchase can be correct when it prevents wasting a second blue resource, and it also serves as a bridge to further tech such as Xeno units. Early DEO can apply pressure while floating little gold.
author: consensus
context: megasupp and shadourow discussing the value of tempo Deadeye in sets with Flame Animus, noting it bridges tech and prevents resource waste.

insight: Thermite opening lines in base-set-only attack sets are: natural Animus for Player 1, and DD DD DBA for Player 2. Thermite lowers the econ of a set by forcing defensive investment, but if it denies 2 absorb it has already paid for itself, so transitioning to Walls is acceptable.
author: megasupp
context: velizar_ asking for guidance on Thermite strategy, with megasupp providing specific opening lines and a cost-benefit heuristic for the unit.

insight: Trying to sustain Thermite purchases indefinitely for full absorb denial becomes increasingly uncomfortable over time, similar to buying Lanceteeth forever. The correct approach is to open with Thermite for efficiency, then stop buying them and transition to attacking rather than forcing continued Thermite purchases.
author: spiritfryer
context: spiritfryer elaborating on Thermite strategy in response to velizar_'s difficulties with the unit.

insight: Mahar Rectifier is not efficient to 'rush' because it is a very expensive absorber requiring a large economy to support. It is barely better than Gauss Cannon to spam, and spamming Gauss Cannons is itself a poor strategy.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of early Mahar Rectifier rushes, with amalloy and shadourow explaining why early heavy Mahar investment is inefficient.

insight: Mahar Rectifier is ineffective offensively when the opponent can neutralize it with a Wall or two Tarsiers. It only provides real offensive value when the opponent already has at least 4 inflexible attack, or runs into a Rhino.
author: velizar_
context: doofmaster and velizar_ discussing conditions under which early Mahar Rectifiers fail to generate value.

insight: In sets where avoiding a Blastforge is desirable (e.g., when Aegis and other high-value green units like Xaetron are present), building two Mahar Rectifiers while skipping the Blastforge entirely can be the correct line.
author: megasupp
context: megasupp noting a specific set condition where double Mahar without Blastforge is correct, in response to discussion of Mahar's weaknesses.

insight: Master Bot plays poorly against Antima Comet; a player can win games they would otherwise lose because the bot mishandles the Antima Comet threat (e.g., using it for only 1 damage when it could deal 10).
author: amalloy
context: amalloy analyzing a new player's replay against Master Bot and noting the player only won due to the bot's poor Antima Comet play.

insight: In RPS (rock-paper-scissors) sets where multiple strategies counter each other, both players should make non-committal moves such as buying more Drones. Over time the RPS typically resolves, and in high-econ games Tatsu Nullifier tends to become the dominant strategy because high economies can support the heavy tech investment while still defending.
author: megasupp
context: megasupp explaining the concept of RPS sets and how Tatsu Nullifier fits into them, in response to a new player's question about strategies.

insight: Tatsu Nullifier is a strong finisher in high-econ games due to its heavy tech requirements, but it is vulnerable to fast pressure units like Grenade Mech that can attack before Tatsu's tech comes online. Grenade Mech is itself not efficient, so it loses to opponents who simply defend with Tarsiers and build economy.
author: megasupp
context: megasupp illustrating the RPS dynamic with Tatsu, Grenade Mech, and Tarsier as an example triangle.

insight: A 4th Engineer is almost never correct; players will be wrong very rarely if they never build a 4th Engineer. Vivid Drone is the most common reason a 4th Engineer could be justified.
author: amalloy
context: New player asking when a 4th Engineer is appropriate; amalloy giving a strong heuristic against it with the only notable exception.

insight: 3 Engineers is unviable in base-set-only (BSO) games and should not be built unless something in the random set specifically incentivizes it.
author: pikachumemes.
context: pikachumemes. advising a new player who was defaulting to 3 Engineers and ending up purely defensive as a result.

insight: Mahar Rectifier functions not just as a green attacker but also as a strong blocker: it absorbs 4 damage, so two Mahars can alternate on defense to consistently absorb 4 each. It also provides 2 threat since the opponent must respect the option of clicking them offensively instead.
author: sano712
context: sano712 explaining Mahar Rectifier's dual offensive/defensive role to a new player who viewed it only as an attacker.

insight: Centurion must be protected from breach at almost any cost, including holding back multiple Drones to defend it, because losing a Centurion to breach is typically game-losing.
author: lyra1712
context: lyra1712 and sano712 analyzing a new player's replay where the Centurion was nearly breached due to overreliance on Walls and ignoring other defensive options.

insight: Rhino should not be used as soak against non-chill threats; instead Rhinos should be deployed against chill threats specifically. Using green resources for Forcefields is preferable to double Rhino soak when there is no chill present.
author: sano712
context: sano712 reviewing a new player's replay where double Rhino was soaked against no chill, then double Wall was bought against chill — the reverse of the correct order.

insight: When Shredder is in the set and the opponent reaches 9 damage (5 Centurion absorb + 4 Shredder soak), Shredder becomes the best consistent soak unit, and players should prioritize soaking on Shredder from that threshold onward.
author: sano712
context: sano712 noting the correct soak priority in a set containing both Centurion and Shredder during replay analysis.

insight: Lanceteeth and Bloodrager without absorb denial have situational value: if the opponent's attack is effectively non-functional (e.g., they have a large surplus of Engineers after Antima Comet), the tempo from absorb denial matters less and prompt soak like Aegis can substitute. However if attack is highly relevant, skipping absorb denial is not worthwhile.
author: pikachumemes.
context: pikachumemes. answering velizar_'s question about whether Lanceteeth/Bloodrager are worth buying without absorb denial when strong prompt soak like Aegis is present.

insight: Xaetron cycling (buying Xaetron and letting it die to generate a permanent attacker) is less valuable when high-quality soak is available in the set, because the defense needed to enable the cycle costs less but the payoff of the cycle also drops in value. The two effects do not fully cancel out; the wall-cycle line remains slightly favorable over alternative defense at standard resource valuations.
author: jamberine
context: Extended discussion between spiritfryer and jamberine quantitatively evaluating the cost-efficiency of Xaetron cycling vs alternative defense in sets with good soak.

insight: Alternating Xaetron and Protoplasm purchases (to cycle Xaetron every other turn) is theoretically appealing but difficult to execute in practice. The trade of buying Protoplasm costs approximately 7GR+1 extra defense now to gain 4 attack and 4 defense next turn, and its value drops when high-quality soak is already available.
author: consensus
context: jamberine and shadourow discussing the Xaetron/Protoplasm alternation strategy after spiritfryer raised the concept.

insight: In Prismata, 'RPS' (rock-paper-scissors) describes sets where multiple viable strategies form a cycle in which each beats at least one other. The correct response to an RPS situation is typically to delay commitment by building more economy (Drones) until the RPS resolves.
author: megasupp
context: megasupp defining and explaining the RPS set concept for a new player asking about strategy types.
